title: Maid Of Honour

jokes.text: David's flat refusal: 'No! No, go away.'
jokes.setup: Moira dramatically summons the family to watch the Crows trailer together
jokes.punchline: David's blunt, childlike 'No! No, go away' — completely deflating Moira's theatrical buildup
jokes.explanation: David's petulant refusal is perfectly in character and economical. The contrast with Moira's grandeur is the joke. Not exceptional but solidly executed character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: John, would you close the curtains? The sun is absolutely murderous.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: The sun is murderous.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's like being inside a tomb. A very bright, very aggressive tomb.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You just asked me to close the curtains.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, and now it's dark. I didn't say it would be pleasant, John.

jokes.text: Moira: 'I don't remember any of this...?' as the trailer plays — it's just a mortgage ad.
jokes.setup: The family has gathered with great ceremony to watch the Crows trailer; Moira is in suspenseful, reverent silence
jokes.punchline: The 'trailer' is a bank mortgage advertisement — Moira's confusion before Johnny explains it's just an ad
jokes.explanation: Great structural joke. After all the ceremony of dimming the room and pressing play, the first thing that comes up is a generic mortgage ad. Moira's confused 'I don't remember any of this' is delightful — she's momentarily questioning her own film. Clean setup and payoff, well-earned.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my God, is that... wait, what is this? What am I watching?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's your movie trailer, Mom.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I know that, but where's my close-up? Where's my moment? This is just... an advertisement.

jokes.text: The trailer buffers and halts immediately after starting. 'It's halted. Someone hit resume.' / 'Just let it buffer.'
jokes.setup: After the ad interruption, the family finally gets to the actual Crows trailer
jokes.punchline: The trailer immediately freezes and buffers, ruining the grand moment; Moira's command 'Someone hit resume' vs Johnny's resigned 'Just let it buffer' captures both personalities
jokes.explanation: The escalating comedy of the trailer experience — first an ad, now buffering — works as a running frustration gag. The contrast between Moira's imperious commands and mundane technological reality is the engine of this sequence.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Come on, come on, come on...

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Is it buffering?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, it's buffering.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, can't you do something about it?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm not a wizard, Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No, but you do have a son who knows computers.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I know computers, I don't know magic.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, this feels like we need magic.

jokes.text: 'Dad, I told you we needed to upgrade the Wi-Fi.' / 'Oh, this is a no way an optimal viewing experience.'
jokes.setup: The trailer continues buffering, ruining Moira's sacred viewing experience
jokes.punchline: Moira's grandiose understatement — calling the buffered phone video a non-optimal 'viewing experience' as if it were a cinema
jokes.explanation: Moira's formal vocabulary applied to a trivial streaming problem is a reliable character joke. 'No way an optimal viewing experience' is perfectly Moira. Functional but part of a running sequence rather than a standout moment.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, this is a technological nightmare of Shakespearean proportions. I'm going to have to insist we return to the pre-digital age where entertainment didn't betray us at every turn.

jokes.text: Alexis tells David she's 'literally stunned' he's getting married, then qualifies: 'Yeah, but like in a good way. Like think of all the people on the planet who find someone, somehow you're one of those people.'
jokes.setup: Alexis finds David's wedding dream book and is moved that his wishes came true
jokes.punchline: Her 'compliment' reveals she genuinely didn't think David would ever find someone — 'somehow you're one of those people' is a loving insult
jokes.explanation: This is warm sibling comedy at its best — Alexis means to be sweet but can't help being accidentally condescending. 'Somehow you're one of those people' is a perfect line that contains multitudes: genuine love and genuine surprise. Both siblings are captured in six words.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, I'm so happy for you. I mean, finding love? That's like... that's actually amazing. For you.

jokes.text: David: 'I don't know how to take that.'
jokes.setup: Alexis has just told him she's stunned he found someone, 'in a good way'
jokes.punchline: David's perfectly calibrated response — acknowledging the ambiguity of the compliment without committing to being offended
jokes.explanation: An efficient punchline to joke #7. David's resigned non-reaction is the correct comedic response — any stronger reaction would have over-explained. Economy at a 9 because it says everything by saying almost nothing.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You know, Patrick is like... he's like a young Orlando Bloom.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Orlando Bloom? Alexis, Patrick looks nothing like Orlando Bloom.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, fine. He's like a young, less handsome Jake Gyllenhaal.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's not a compliment.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: He's like if Chad Michael Murray and a piece of whole wheat toast had a baby.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why would you say that?

jokes.text: Alexis: 'I mean, you didn't end up marrying JC Chasez or Jenna Elfman. But I actually feel like Patrick is kind of the best of both of them.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is trying to say Patrick is the ideal partner for David
jokes.punchline: Her frame of reference is two completely random early-2000s celebrities — JC Chasez from *NSYNC and Jenna Elfman — as David's apparent past aspirations
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's first great joke. The specificity of 'JC Chasez or Jenna Elfman' is absurdly perfect — those are exactly the kind of oddly specific celebrity crushes a wealthy early-2000s teenager would have, and the pairing is genuinely unexpected and funny. 'Patrick is kind of the best of both of them' is a perfectly weird compliment. High quotability, rewatch value because you want to process the pairing. Character-specific to Alexis's particular brand of name-dropping.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, so you're saying Patrick is like JC Chasez?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Because honestly, that tracks.

jokes.text: Moira bursts in referencing 'JC Chasez': 'Well, if it isn't our very own little JC Chasez.' Patrick: 'Sorry. Who?' Moira/David quickly deflect: 'Ooh, we've been over that. Um, we should probably go.'
jokes.setup: Alexis just compared Patrick to JC Chasez; Moira enters having overheard
jokes.punchline: Patrick has no idea who JC Chasez is, immediately undercutting the compliment; the Rose family scrambles to move past it
jokes.explanation: Smart immediate callback — Patrick's genuine ignorance of JC Chasez is funnier than if he'd played along. The family panic to redirect is also well-executed. Rewatch bonus because it rewards paying attention to the earlier joke.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, you asked Ronnie to be your maid of honor?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Ronnie? Who hates you?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: She doesn't hate him.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Patrick, she literally told him to drop dead.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: That's just how she talks.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: I do hate him.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Oh, where are my favourite groom and David off to?' — implying David is not her favourite.
jokes.setup: Moira asks where Patrick and David are going
jokes.punchline: The phrasing 'my favourite groom and David' — listing David separately and implicitly second — suggests Patrick is now more beloved than her own son
jokes.explanation: A perfectly economical Moira line. The joke is entirely in the phrasing — she doesn't even realize (or care) that she's implied David is not her favourite. Pure character comedy, very Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, Stevie? Like, the famous Stevie, or local Stevie?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's just Stevie.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, but there's levels to it, David.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'David, you better be talking about Stevie Nicks.'
jokes.setup: David says he asked 'Stevie' to be his maid of honour
jokes.punchline: Alexis assumes — and sincerely hopes — it's Stevie Nicks, revealing her celebrity-obsessed frame of reference
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect one-liner. 'You better be talking about Stevie Nicks' captures Alexis completely — her celebrity name-dropping is so ingrained that her first interpretation of 'Stevie' is Stevie Nicks. Economy is excellent; the joke does everything in one sentence. High quotability.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Fine, go ahead and talk to Stevie. But please try not to make this about you.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, everything is about me.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, today is ruining my day.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Imagine what I could do with your bachelor party, David. Diplo still sends me nudes.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is lobbying to take over maid-of-honour duties from Stevie
jokes.punchline: Her key credential: that Diplo (the famous DJ) still sends her unsolicited nudes — stated proudly as a logistical asset
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke. 'Diplo still sends me nudes' is a perfect Alexis line — absurd celebrity name-drop, comically specific (it's nudes, not texts), and used as a genuine professional credential for bachelor party planning. The structure is flawless: 'Imagine what I could do' sets up some kind of impressive boast, and the punchline completely subverts expectations. Character integration is a 9.5 — only Alexis Rose could say this line. Highly quotable, rewatch worthy, the room would erupt.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I can totally help with the wedding planning. I know a celebrity DJ who's amazing.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: He did send me some nude photos, but that was just... a thing that happened.

jokes.text: David corrects Alexis: 'It's a DJ.' — as if explaining that a famous DJ sending nudes is still somehow an odd flex.
jokes.setup: Alexis says 'Diplo still sends me nudes' as a credential
jokes.punchline: David's only objection is clarifying what Diplo does for a living — not the nude part — delivered with complete exhaustion
jokes.explanation: Possibly the best secondary reaction in the episode. David's 'It's a DJ' only addresses the most trivial part of what Alexis said — the nudes are so normalized in their world that his only correction is occupational. The economy is a 10: three words, perfect beat. This works as a callback punchline that deepens the original joke considerably.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, Johnny, you're one to talk about bad investments. You once paid $40,000 for a timeshare in a strip mall.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That was different.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Was it? Because I'm pretty sure you can't even get a decent footlong there.

jokes.text: Roland's surprise at the motel condition: 'What were you expecting, Johnny? I don't throw good money down on lemons. Who am I, you buying your car?'
jokes.setup: Johnny is surprised the motel rooms are in decent shape
jokes.punchline: Roland turns it into a dig at Johnny's apparent history of bad car purchases — 'Who am I, you buying your car?'
jokes.explanation: A competent Roland dig — he turns a straightforward compliment into a self-serving insult of Johnny. The car reference is a nice specific detail. Functional character comedy, not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, what do we got here? Oh baby, that's a lot of cash!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, we need to call the police. There's a gun.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa. Hold on. I'm thinking we split this down the middle, you take the money, I'll take the gun.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's not how this works, Roland.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: What? We're partners! Fifty-fifty!

jokes.text: Roland finds money in the room: 'Oh, look... the maid forgot to pick up her tip.' — then reveals it's a bag full of money.
jokes.setup: Roland spots something the maid left behind and frames it as a forgotten tip
jokes.punchline: It's not a small tip but a literal bag full of cash — Roland's casual framing of a clearly criminal discovery as a routine maid oversight
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection — 'maid forgot to pick up her tip' is a perfect understatement for stumbling on what is clearly criminal proceeds. The structure works well: casual observation → reveal of alarming reality.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: They'll be back for their things. People always come back for their things.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, my father never came back for his things, and he left in '82.

jokes.text: Roland: 'Yeah, it's kinda a generous tip when you consider she didn't clean up this garbage and this loaded gun.'
jokes.setup: Johnny asks if it's a bag of money
jokes.punchline: Roland matter-of-factly connects the tip, the garbage, AND the loaded gun in the same sentence — as if finding a loaded gun is a minor inconvenience on par with uncleaned trash
jokes.explanation: Roland's ability to normalize a loaded gun as a bullet point in a maid critique is genuinely funny. The escalation from 'garbage' to 'loaded gun' delivered with the same flat affect is classic Roland character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: The coffee is still hot.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Global warming.

jokes.text: Roland: 'Maybe Benny and Betty left this here, and we just inherited a bag of money and a gun. If we're gonna split it up, I call the money.'
jokes.setup: Johnny concludes someone has been squatting illegally
jokes.punchline: Roland's first instinct is not alarm but pragmatic division of criminal proceeds — 'I call the money'
jokes.explanation: Roland's cheerful willingness to just pocket criminal proceeds is perfectly in character. 'I call the money' is a great punchline — childlike dibs-claiming logic applied to what is clearly crime scene evidence.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: We should stake out the criminal. I'll go undercover as a woman.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Why would you need to go undercover as a woman?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Criminals don't expect women. They're not looking for a woman.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Roland, that's a rather reductive assumption about criminal behavior.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I don't know, Moira. I'm just saying criminals have gender biases.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So you're saying we should challenge those stereotypes and recognize that criminals are equal opportunity offenders regardless of gender?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Exactly! Thank you, David. Criminals come in all genders. We need to be more progressive about this.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Then explain why the coffee is still hot?' / Roland: 'It's called global warming, Johnny.'
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to prove the squatter was recently there by pointing to hot coffee as evidence
jokes.punchline: Roland's non-sequitur explanation: global warming accounts for the hot coffee
jokes.explanation: A genuinely excellent joke. Johnny's detective-style hot coffee deduction is perfectly set up as a logical argument, and Roland's pivot to 'global warming' is a spectacular non sequitur that also reveals Roland's complete scientific misunderstanding. The added joke is the implicit accusation that Johnny doesn't believe in climate change ('Why do you strike me as one of those people'). Economy is excellent — the structure is immaculate. One of the episode's highest-craft moments.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I used thaumaturgy for the special effects.

jokes.text: Roland: 'Why do you strike me as one of those people that doesn't believe in that.' — immediately after using global warming as an excuse
jokes.setup: Roland has just invoked global warming to explain hot coffee in a criminal's motel room
jokes.punchline: He immediately accuses Johnny of being a climate denier — despite Roland being the one who just misused the concept
jokes.explanation: A great secondary beat to the global warming joke. Roland has just deployed global warming as a nonsensical excuse, then pivots to accuse Johnny of not believing in it — completely unaware of the irony. This captures Roland's particular brand of oblivious self-righteousness perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The imaginators really outdid themselves with the special effects.

jokes.text: Cut from the motel crime scene to the Crows movie trailer: 'Well, we best be returning to the lab, Nathaniel.' But the buffered video suddenly plays in the middle of the stakeout scene.
jokes.setup: The Rose family has been struggling to get the Crows trailer to play all scene
jokes.punchline: The trailer finally plays at a completely different moment — after everyone has moved on
jokes.explanation: Good structural comedy — the trailer starts playing as a separate scene, cleverly resolving the buffering gag from earlier by having the video start on its own terms. Works as a callback payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I was wearing a prosthetic beak. A human beak, if you will.

jokes.text: Crows trailer scene: 'It's getting dark.' / 'But it's the middle of the afternoon, must be a solar eclipse.' / 'That's not an eclipse, Nathaniel! Those are birds! Ahhh!'
jokes.setup: The Crows movie trailer plays — we hear dramatic dialogue about darkness
jokes.punchline: The twist: the 'eclipse' is actually a swarm of crows — a horror-movie reveal that is also genuinely campy and funny in its earnestness
jokes.explanation: The Crows trailer content is comedic in its B-movie earnestness. The eclipse-to-birds reveal is a classic horror trope played completely straight, which is itself funny given the context. Functional absurdist comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, the response has been quite effusive. The accolades are arriving at a pace I can barely process. I'm not one to engage in self-aggrandizement, but one would be remiss to ignore the overwhelming tide of approbation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Of course, I suppose there may be some small corners of the critical establishment that have yet to weigh in. One does hope they won't be too devastated by their tardy arrival to the consensus.

jokes.text: 'Mrs. Rose, was that you the whole time?' / 'Indeed it was. Thanks in large part to the thaumaturgy of our special effects department. Stunning, isn't it? What a human beak can do?'
jokes.setup: The trailer has just ended with Moira's crow transformation being the big reveal
jokes.punchline: Moira describes the VFX as 'thaumaturgy' and asks 'What a human beak can do?' with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: 'Thaumaturgy' (meaning miracle-working) for what is presumably low-budget prosthetics is gloriously overwrought. 'What a human beak can do' is a wonderfully strange sentence that only Moira would say with pride. The character integration is outstanding.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, it was... it was very long.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: And there were certainly a lot of... people in it.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I mean, you could definitely tell that someone made it.

jokes.text: Twyla: 'Mrs. Rose, was that you the whole time?' Moira: 'Indeed it was. Thanks in large part to the thaumaturgy of our special effects department. Stunning, isn't it? What a human beak can do?'
jokes.setup: The Crows trailer has just played — Twyla is processing that Moira was in it
jokes.punchline: Moira credits the 'thaumaturgy' of special effects, then pivots to the specific achievement of 'what a human beak can do' — implying she wore a prosthetic beak
jokes.explanation: 'What a human beak can do' is a wonderful line — 'human beak' is an inherently absurd combination of words that raises more questions than it answers. Moira's vocabulary ('thaumaturgy') elevates the absurdism. High quotability for the beak line specifically.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, you're really glowing today. Have you been doing something different with your face?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh Jocelyn, thank you! I've actually been trying this new jade roller technique. I'm so glad it's noticeable!

jokes.text: 'No, I think three times through is the sweet spot.'
jokes.setup: Moira asks if they should watch the trailer again
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's measured response — implying they've already watched it three times and she's been counting down to when she can stop — is a perfectly delivered piece of suffering
jokes.explanation: Jocelyn's flat 'three times through is the sweet spot' is a great deadpan. It implies she has been suffering through repeat viewings and has arrived at a diplomatic maximum. The economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Were the crows nice?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Oh, well, that reminds me of the time a pelican tried to steal my sandwich at the beach. I was wearing my mother's good cardigan, and it had a button missing, so the pelican thought my exposed sternum was a fish. It wasn't. But try explaining that to a pelican.

jokes.text: Twyla: 'What if it gets nominated for an Oscar?' Moira: 'Twyla! No, we must keep the carriage in the wake of the mare. Golden Globe, perhaps, but I won't let myself envisage that.'
jokes.setup: The group is celebrating the trailer; Twyla excitedly mentions an Oscar
jokes.punchline: Moira feigns restraint — correcting Twyla's Oscar mention while privately coveting a Golden Globe, delivered with theatrical self-discipline
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Moira — she pretends not to indulge award fantasies while immediately fantasizing about a Golden Globe. 'I won't let myself envisage that' while clearly envisaging it is excellent character irony. 'Keep the carriage in the wake of the mare' is lovely Moira malapropism territory.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God, is that a flight attendant uniform? Stevie, you look like you're about to explain the safety features of a Boeing 747 to a cabin full of people who definitely aren't listening.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: David...

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm just saying, the only thing missing is a forced smile and the dead look of someone who's explained oxygen masks for the thousandth time. Very 'Pan Am' meets 'slowly losing the will to live.'

jokes.text: Jocelyn's tepid review: 'Well... it looked just like a real movie.' Moira: 'Now there is the quote for the sizzle reel!'
jokes.setup: Moira solicits Jocelyn's opinion after universal praise from Twyla and others
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's damning-with-faint-praise 'it looked just like a real movie' — and Moira's delusional embrace of it as a quotable blurb
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured two-beat joke. 'It looked just like a real movie' is an exquisitely damning non-compliment — the lowest possible bar of praise. Moira claiming it for the sizzle reel is perfect: she's so desperate for validation that she spins genuine faint praise into marketing copy. High craft, strong impact.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I got the job as a flight attendant.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I know what you're thinking. 'Stevie, you hate people.' And you're right. But apparently that's not a dealbreaker for the airline industry.

jokes.text: Jocelyn tries again: 'Oh... it's-it's neat!' / Moira: '"Neat."' / Jocelyn: 'To see you in a... in a real movie.'
jokes.setup: Moira's obvious disappointment at Jocelyn's 'looks like a real movie' prompts Jocelyn to try harder
jokes.punchline: She lands on 'neat' — even worse than before; Moira's single-word echo of 'Neat' conveys volumes of withering contempt
jokes.explanation: The callback doubles down on the cringe — Jocelyn's attempt to improve her review lands on 'neat,' which is catastrophically worse. Moira's deadpan single-word repetition 'Neat.' is pitch-perfect: all the contempt in the world without a single additional syllable.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Even the owner is suing his own company

jokes.text: Twyla, while leaving: 'Were the crows nice? My uncle had a parrot that just kept asking me to take my bra off.'
jokes.setup: The scene is wrapping up and everyone is heading to rehearsal
jokes.punchline: Twyla's completely non-sequitur contribution: her uncle's sexually inappropriate parrot — delivered as if this is a completely normal thing to share
jokes.explanation: Twyla's exit line is excellent. The non-sequitur is perfectly timed — arriving as everyone is leaving, so it lands and then the scene ends before anyone can process it. The detail of the parrot asking her to take her bra off is specific, weird, and deeply Twyla — she raises the darkly odd detail with complete innocence. High originality, excellent character integration, rewatch bonus for the perfect timing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, let me think about that for a second.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Everything.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'Isn't she though?' / Alexis: 'Okay. That's funny. More of that. But aim it at David.'
jokes.setup: David says Stevie will feel like she's doing him a favour by stepping aside
jokes.punchline: Patrick drily confirms Stevie IS doing David a favour — and Alexis approves but redirects his wit toward David
jokes.explanation: Patrick's 'Isn't she though?' is sharp and well-timed — his dry wit cutting through David's self-important scheming. Alexis's stage-manager follow-up ('More of that. But aim it at David.') adds a second layer. Good economy across both lines.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: These are artisanal potato chips. They're made with spring water that's been blessed by a shaman in Peru.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, that's just regular water and potatoes.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, the shaman part really sells it, doesn't it?

jokes.text: Stevie appears in a flight attendant uniform. David: 'You do know that Pan Am was cancelled after a season, right?'
jokes.setup: Stevie shows up unexpectedly in a flight attendant's uniform
jokes.punchline: David's instant read — the uniform looks like it's from the short-lived ABC show 'Pan Am,' a dig at both Stevie and the failed series
jokes.explanation: David's cultural reference is perfectly him — he can't see a flight attendant uniform without making a TV reference. 'Cancelled after a season' is a nice extra dig. Good economy, character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, well, well. Look who it is. You know, Johnny, I gotta say, I really admire your confidence. Most people would take one look at that and assume it was a man. But not you. You just went right ahead and assumed it was a man anyway.

jokes.text: 'So how safe can the plane be if I'm the one in charge of saving people's lives?'
jokes.setup: Stevie reveals she just became a flight attendant less than a week ago
jokes.punchline: Stevie turns her own incompetence into a public safety concern with complete candor
jokes.explanation: Stevie's self-deprecating dark humor is her signature. 'If I'm the one in charge of saving people's lives' is a genuinely good joke — the implication that passengers should be worried is both funny and a subtle dig at the airline. Alexis's response ('Yikes. Not safe.') is the perfect beat.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You gave me a look. A look that said 'I have seen the film, and I have decided that it is not for me.' It was a look of such casual indifference that it made me feel as though I had been slowly lowered into a vat of ice water while being told that my life's work was merely adequate.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'They only fly to Hoboken, Windsor and South Dakota.' Patrick: 'I've actually heard South Dakota is quite scenic.'
jokes.setup: Stevie laments the airline's limited destinations as evidence of the job's worthlessness
jokes.punchline: Patrick earnestly defends South Dakota — the most deflating possible response to Stevie's complaint
jokes.explanation: Patrick's genuinely earnest South Dakota defense is a good character beat — he's the wholesome counterpoint to everyone else's cynicism. The specificity of Hoboken, Windsor, and South Dakota as the three destinations is funny on its own.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I'm sorry, but I found Mike Myers to be quite charming in the Austin Powers films. Very silly, but charming.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Did you know that Larry Air has 18 ongoing lawsuits? One of which is from Larry himself.'
jokes.setup: Stevie is cataloguing all the reasons her new job is terrible
jokes.punchline: The airline has 18 lawsuits — but the funniest is that one is from the airline's own founder, Larry
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke. The setup '18 ongoing lawsuits' is already funny, but the punchline — 'one of which is from Larry himself' — is genuinely surprising and perfectly structured. The implication that the airline's founder is suing his own company raises enormous questions and is funnier for leaving them unanswered. High originality, economy is excellent, highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You know, if you're going to an event, you really need to commit to a look. I once met, dated, and broke up with someone at a gala. It was very efficient.

jokes.text: Alexis's manipulation: 'Oh no, babe. It sounds like you have so much on your plate already, on top of your bridesmaids duties. Are you sure you want that much stress?'
jokes.setup: Alexis is trying to get Stevie to give up the maid-of-honour role by making her feel overwhelmed
jokes.punchline: The transparent manipulation — she cares not at all about Stevie's stress and entirely about getting the role back — is very Alexis
jokes.explanation: The manipulation is obvious, which is the joke — Alexis isn't subtle. But it works because Stevie almost falls for it before catching herself. Functional character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I've planned parties in Monaco, Ibiza, and this one time in international waters where there were literally no laws.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'As crazy as this sounds, I'm actually looking forward to that. Feel like it's the only job I can't mess up at this point.'
jokes.setup: Alexis tries to convince Stevie the maid-of-honour role would be too stressful on top of everything
jokes.punchline: Stevie genuinely looks forward to it — because she's messed up everything else, a wedding feels like the one thing she can't ruin
jokes.explanation: Good Stevie moment — her logic that the maid-of-honour role is safer than her professional life is simultaneously sad and funny. Warm character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, I'm not great at planning things. I once planned a surprise party and forgot to invite the person.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Also, I've never actually been to a wedding sober, so I'm not sure what the maid of honor even does.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: But sure, I can definitely do it. How hard can it be?

jokes.text: Roland about the vending machine: 'Do you realize that this soda is made with 99% real drink. You can't argue with that kind of math.'
jokes.setup: Roland is excited about the motel's Drink Tyme Orange Sodas from the vending machine
jokes.punchline: '99% real drink' — an entirely meaningless marketing claim that Roland accepts as rigorous proof of quality. 'Real drink' is not an ingredient.
jokes.explanation: '99% real drink' is a brilliantly absurd joke. The label is obvious nonsense — 'real drink' is not a substance — but Roland takes it as a genuine quality metric. His 'you can't argue with that kind of math' doubles down by treating this non-claim as mathematical evidence. Excellent originality and character integration — only Roland would find this persuasive.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I've been unemployed twice in the last six months. That's a personal record.

jokes.text: Roland justifies eating vending machine snacks: 'Well, why do you think I got these snacks? I mean we could be on this stakeout for days, maybe weeks.'
jokes.setup: Johnny tells Roland to focus on their actual task
jokes.punchline: Roland reveals he's thinking of this as a potentially multi-week stakeout — wildly overestimating the scope of their situation
jokes.explanation: Roland's grandiose planning for a motel stakeout is on-brand. 'Days, maybe weeks' is a nice escalation of the stakes in his imagination. Functional.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Patrick, you look amazing! It's like James Bond, but, like, accessible.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Thank you.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's giving very 007, but make it small-town Canadian.

jokes.text: Roland, watching the squatter from hiding: 'It is a man. Your sexist instincts were right as usual.'
jokes.setup: Roland earlier corrected Johnny's 'the guy' by saying 'or gal, this is the 21st century.' The squatter turns out to be a man.
jokes.punchline: Roland cheerfully credits Johnny's 'sexist instincts' — calling correct-gender-assumption 'sexist' while simultaneously noting it was right
jokes.explanation: Smart callback. Roland had performatively corrected Johnny's masculine pronoun, then is forced to acknowledge the correction was unnecessary — but still calls it 'sexist instincts.' The contradiction is perfectly Roland. Good structure as a callback joke.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Now look, friend, we don't want any trouble here. This man here, he's what we call a 'Professor Prude' — uptight, doesn't know how to have a good time.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland!

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: What? I'm trying to help!

jokes.text: Jocelyn: 'My first boyfriend took me to a Michael Myers movie and I never got over it.' Moira: 'Not "The Love Guru"?'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn explains her horror-movie aversion stems from a bad experience with Michael Myers
jokes.punchline: Moira assumes 'Michael Myers' means Mike Myers (the comedian/actor), referencing The Love Guru — an extremely specific and odd reference that reveals Moira's Hollywood-centric frame of reference
jokes.explanation: Exceptional joke. The Michael Myers/Mike Myers confusion is a classic homophone setup — but the specific choice of 'The Love Guru' (a notorious Mike Myers flop) is what elevates it. Moira's first assumption is not Halloween but a critically reviled comedy, which tells us everything about her world. 'Not The Love Guru?' is perfectly economical — two words plus a title that does enormous comedic work. Near the top of the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Prosecutor
dialogue.line: I've put away a lot of criminals in my time.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, that's great. I mean, not great for the criminals, but great for, you know, law and order and keeping the streets safe for regular folks like me.

dialogue.speaker: Prosecutor
dialogue.line: Thank you.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Although, I guess 'criminals' is a pretty loaded term. I mean, they're people too, right? People who maybe made some mistakes, or were victims of circumstance, or the system—

dialogue.speaker: Prosecutor
dialogue.line: They murdered people.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Right. Well, then lock 'em up.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'I once hooked up with three different GQ Men of the Year. At the GQ Men of the Year party.'
jokes.setup: They're discussing bow ties for Patrick's wedding suit; Alexis offers fashion credentials
jokes.punchline: Her credentials are: having hooked up with three GQ Men of the Year — at the event itself — as expertise in how bow ties photograph
jokes.explanation: Classic Alexis credential-dropping — her qualification for bow tie opinions is a sexual résumé at a prestige event. 'Three different' is the key word that makes it land — not one, not two, but three. The clarification 'at the GQ Men of the Year party' adds specificity and implies impressive logistics. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That was... a very nice thing you did, Roland.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Just nice? It was spectacular!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, well, it was quite good.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Quite good?! I need you to be more specific. Was it the best thing you've ever seen? Better than television?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I... suppose it was well-executed.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well-executed?! I need to know if it was better than my last accomplishment. And the one before that. And rank all my accomplishments in order from best to worst, with detailed explanations!

jokes.text: David: 'I found a gorgeous ryokan in Hakone with a hot spring-fed onsen on the balcony. Private Chef optional.'
jokes.setup: David claims he's 'super easy' about bachelor party plans
jokes.punchline: He then immediately describes a specific luxury Japanese inn with a private chef — his idea of 'easy'
jokes.explanation: David's self-declared easiness immediately contradicted by extremely specific luxury demands is classic. 'Private Chef optional' is a wonderful qualifier — the 'optional' implies it's still on the table. Good character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I have written a review of the film. Moira, I hope this meets your cinematographical standards.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, Jocelyn, how thoughtful of you.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: The film was very... uh... cromulent. Yes, very cromulent indeed. The acting was positively... flibbertigibbety.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Those aren't real words, Jocelyn.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, I tried my best.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Hot tip: it often makes for less stress if you just plan it yourself and then tell the bride after.' Stevie: 'Okay. Stevie, not hot on that tip.'
jokes.setup: Alexis offers Stevie bachelor party planning advice
jokes.punchline: David immediately shuts it down — Alexis's 'hot tip' is to exclude the groom from planning his own party
jokes.explanation: Alexis's instinct to plan around rather than with the person being celebrated is perfectly her. David's correction ('not hot on that tip') — using her own format against her — is a nice small touch.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The crows have accepted me on set. One even tried to mate with me.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Oh my God, Stevie. I get why you and everyone else would be asking that question, but the answer is... like maybe?'
jokes.setup: Stevie is handing Alexis exactly what she was scheming to get — the maid-of-honour role
jokes.punchline: Alexis, in the moment of victory, suddenly falters — 'like maybe?' — revealing her sudden uncertainty undercuts her entire scheme
jokes.explanation: Excellent structural subversion. Everything has been built toward Alexis reclaiming the role, and at the moment of triumph she says 'like maybe?' — a perfect revelation of her deeper crisis. The comedic misdirection serves the character development, which is the show's strength.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, I have a much better job for you at the wedding. I need someone to handle all the floral arrangements, coordinate with the vendors, manage the timeline...

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, David, that sounds like... a lot of responsibility. What if I just, like, held a clipboard and looked concerned?

jokes.text: Stevie: 'What am I doing?' / Alexis: 'Well, we can just crossfade responsibilities.' / Stevie: 'No. What am I doing with my life.'
jokes.setup: Stevie's 'what am I doing?' seems like it's about the maid-of-honour logistics
jokes.punchline: It's actually an existential crisis — 'What am I doing with my life' — a larger, more devastating question that Alexis's tactical response completely misread
jokes.explanation: Smart misdirection — Alexis's 'crossfade responsibilities' response is perfectly timed to be maximally unhelpful. The pivot from a task question to an existential one is well-structured and the comedy works because Alexis's tactical answer is so comically inappropriate to the real question.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, so I could be the flower girl, and then during the ceremony I could slip into the back, change into a bridesmaid dress, and walk back out? And then maybe during the reception I could do a quick change into like a hostess situation? I mean, I've done wardrobe changes faster than that at Fashion Week.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You're not even listening to my vision.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Okay, all of what you're saying may be very, very true. Which is why I will not let you give up this job.'
jokes.setup: Stevie is having a breakdown about being unemployed in her thirties
jokes.punchline: Alexis agrees everything is terrible — and uses that as the reason Stevie must keep the maid-of-honour job. The logic is deliberately circular: things are bad, so don't make changes.
jokes.explanation: Alexis's advice is wonderfully absurd — validate the crisis, then use it to reinforce the status quo. It's caring in intent but makes no logical sense. Good character integration for Alexis's particular style of supportiveness.

jokes.text: Patrick emerges in a suit. Alexis: 'Oh my God. Since when did James Bond shop at Elm Glenn?'
jokes.setup: Patrick comes out of the fitting room in a wedding suit
jokes.punchline: Alexis's James Bond comparison — immediately undercut by 'Elm Glenn,' the name of a presumably modest local clothing store
jokes.explanation: Two-part joke that works beautifully: the James Bond compliment is a genuine compliment, made funnier by the specific store name 'Elm Glenn' — which sounds deeply provincial. The contrast between the glamour of the compliment and the mundanity of the location is the joke.

jokes.text: Stevie asked for tie opinion — long pause of her considering, humming — then just: 'Hm. Hm. Which one is your favourite.'
jokes.setup: David asks Stevie for her maid-of-honour opinion on which tie Patrick should wear
jokes.punchline: After appearing to give it serious thought, Stevie just bounces the question back — she has no idea and won't pretend to
jokes.explanation: A great Stevie moment — she performs consideration, then completely deflects. The pause is the joke; the 'which one is your favourite' is perfect because it's exactly what she was just asked. The visual of her humming while clearly having no opinion is strong character comedy.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'Yes. Can we just get married already? I'm kidding, I want pageantry that takes months and months to plan.' / David: 'Of course you do.'
jokes.setup: Patrick is standing in his suit and everyone approves
jokes.punchline: Patrick immediately self-corrects his 'can we just get married' with an enthusiastic declaration that he wants months of planning — which is entirely about David and completely accepted by David
jokes.explanation: Patrick's self-correction reveals how well he knows David — he knows the pageantry matters enormously and he's fully signed up for it. 'Of course you do' from David is warm and confirms he absolutely does want all of that. A sweet character beat that's also funny.

jokes.text: Johnny introduces himself to the squatter: '...and this is my partner... Partner. He calls me... No we actually just met.'
jokes.setup: Johnny and Roland are confronting an armed squatter; Johnny tries to explain their relationship
jokes.punchline: He can't explain what Roland is — 'partner' doesn't fit, he tries to say Roland calls him something, then settles on 'we actually just met' which is obviously also false
jokes.explanation: Johnny's panicked inability to define his relationship with Roland — even after years of knowing each other — is good cringe comedy. The landing on 'we actually just met' is a delightful non-solution to the problem.

jokes.text: Roland: 'And by the way, whatever you're doing is A-okay with me.' — to an armed squatter who may be a criminal
jokes.setup: Johnny is trying to delicately find out what the squatter is doing there
jokes.punchline: Roland immediately preemptively absolves all potential crimes — 'A-okay with me' — as a tension-defusing strategy that also reveals his complete moral flexibility
jokes.explanation: Roland's instinct to blanket-pardon whatever crimes are being committed is funny and very on-brand. The casual moral flexibility contrasts nicely with Johnny's more principled discomfort.

jokes.text: The squatter explains he's a prosecutor keeping a low profile. Roland: 'Or gals.' Squatter: 'No, never women. It's always safest...'
jokes.setup: The prosecutor explains he puts away dangerous criminals, using 'guys'
jokes.punchline: Roland automatically gender-corrects to 'or gals' — and the prosecutor flatly says 'No, never women' — undermining Roland's performative inclusivity while making a disturbing point
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to Roland's earlier gender-correction. He does the same thing again ('or gals') and is again immediately shut down — this time in a darker way that implies something sinister about who the prosecutor targets. The reversal of Roland's woke correction being denied twice is a running gag with a dark twist.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'That explains what that big ole bag was for.' Squatter: 'How do you know I have a big old bag?' Johnny: 'I didn't know. I don't know anything about a bag.'
jokes.setup: Johnny has just accidentally revealed they went through the squatter's room
jokes.punchline: His panicked immediate denial — 'I didn't know. I don't know anything about a bag.' — while obviously lying
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment — Johnny's slip and immediate desperate cover are a classic sitcom beat, but well-executed. The escalation of the squatter getting increasingly suspicious while Johnny digs himself deeper works.

jokes.text: 'Well, Johnny, you still wanna call the cops?' / 'Okay, you were right on this one, Roland.' / 'I think this was a pretty important one to be right on.'
jokes.setup: They have just successfully navigated keeping an armed prosecutor as a paying guest
jokes.punchline: John admits Roland was right; Roland responds with 'I think this was a pretty important one to be right on' — understating the danger while claiming maximum credit
jokes.explanation: Functional button on the motel subplot. Roland's self-satisfaction is in character and the understatement ('pretty important one') works. Not exceptional but a solid scene closer.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Do go on.' — after Jocelyn begins to read a review she wrote about the trailer.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn says she has something positive to say about the trailer
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'Do go on' — leaning forward with hungry, barely contained anticipation — is a beat of pure comedic character
jokes.explanation: The beat works primarily as physical comedy — Moira's contained hunger for praise expressed in two words. Economy is near-perfect, but the joke requires the performance to fully land.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Who wrote that?' / Jocelyn: 'I did.' / Moira: 'You? You did.' (pause) 'Oh wow... Jocelyn, thank you.' (pause) 'Nothing about me or my performance?'
jokes.setup: Moira is moved by Jocelyn's glowing critical prose
jokes.punchline: After a genuine moment of gratitude, Moira immediately pivots to disappointment that the review was about the film's atmosphere rather than her specific performance
jokes.explanation: The pivot from genuine gratitude to 'Nothing about me or my performance?' is a perfect character beat. Moira is touched — for about three seconds — before realizing she wasn't specifically praised. The beat timing is crucial and the joke works because we briefly believed she might just accept the compliment.

jokes.text: Jocelyn: 'I really liked your feathers!' Moira: 'Surprise. They are real. 1200 of them hand-sewn into my costume. The live crows on set welcomed me as their own. One even tried to mate.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn offers a positive specific observation about Moira's costume feathers
jokes.punchline: Moira reveals: real feathers (1200, hand-sewn), the crows treated her as one of them, and one tried to mate with her — each revelation more alarming than the last
jokes.explanation: Outstanding escalation joke. Jocelyn's innocent compliment ('I liked your feathers!') triggers a cascade of escalating revelations: the feathers are real, there are 1200, they're hand-sewn, the crows accepted Moira as one of them — and then the crowning punchline that a crow tried to mate with her. Each beat lands before the next arrives. 'One even tried to mate' is delivered as a point of pride, not alarm — which is deeply Moira. One of the episode's best jokes.

jokes.text: David to Alexis: 'Wow. Who are you?' Alexis: 'I know, right? Like what's happening to me?' David: 'You're going soft.' Alexis: 'Ugh.' David: 'But don't worry. I won't tell anyone.'
jokes.setup: Alexis has just chosen to let Stevie keep the maid-of-honour role instead of taking it back
jokes.punchline: Both siblings treat genuine kindness as something shameful and secret — 'I won't tell anyone' about Alexis's goodness
jokes.explanation: Warm character comedy. Both Roses treating kindness as a secret vice is funny and true to who they are and who they're becoming. 'I won't tell anyone' is a nice economy of expression for their shared emotional reticence.

jokes.text: David: 'A simple yes or no is fine. No need to pull focus.' / Alexis: 'I do.' / then: 'Although now that I think about it, I could also be the flower girl...'
jokes.setup: David has asked Alexis to give him away at the wedding — a deeply sincere moment
jokes.punchline: Alexis immediately accepts, then immediately tries to negotiate being BOTH the person giving him away AND the flower girl
jokes.explanation: The warm sibling moment is immediately undercut by Alexis's instinct to maximize her own role. Her flower-girl proposal — coming out first in 'a gown made entirely of flowers,' looping back, changing outfits — is wonderfully specific and self-aggrandizing. Very Alexis.

jokes.text: Alexis describes her flower girl plan: 'Like come out first, wearing a gown made entirely of flowers... Just walk down the aisle, and loop back... and then quick outfit change, and we're up.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is proposing to also be the flower girl at David's wedding
jokes.punchline: Her detailed plan involves a full gown of flowers, a theatrical loop back down the aisle, AND a quick costume change — at someone else's wedding
jokes.explanation: Alexis's wedding-within-a-wedding vision is perfectly absurd. The 'loop back' is what makes it — she's choreographed a full theatrical entrance at her brother's wedding. Character integration is excellent — this is entirely Alexis.

tmdb_overview: Moira shares the newly released “Crows” movie trailer with the Jazzagals. Johnny and Roland find a suspicious bag of money at the new motel.
insight: Wedding prep chaos yields 43 jokes in 41 minutes—character comedy carries the load.
